title: Internal Market Making

content.statement: Scarce internal resources should flow toward highest-value uses, not the loudest politics.
content.question: When two priorities in your business compete for the same time, cash, or attention, what's the rule for who wins?
content.equation: IMM = (Speed × Accuracy × Match Quality) ÷ Political Distortion
sop: List the scarce resources people actually compete for in your business, time, budget, your personal attention, key equipment.
Write one explicit rule for how each gets allocated when there's a conflict (e.g., highest revenue impact wins, first-come-first-served, ROI threshold).
Share the rule with your team so allocation decisions stop being political and start being predictable.
Rate Speed, Accuracy, and Match Quality 1-10 for your last resource decision, and rate Political Distortion 1-10 (see calculation, page 6)
Revisit the rules quarterly, what made sense at 2 people won't hold at 10.
content.explainer: Scarce resources need a pricing mechanism or they get allocated by whoever complains loudest.
questions: When two priorities in your business compete for the same time, cash, or attention, what's the rule for who wins?
Was your last resource allocation decision made by a rule, or by whoever pushed hardest?
If two team leads both need the same budget or your time next month, who decides, and how?

definitions.def: Acting as the mechanism that matches supply and demand for a scarce resource, deciding who gets it when more than one party wants it.

content.equation_example: Rate Speed, Accuracy, and Match Quality 1-10 each for your last resource decision, then rate Political Distortion 1-10. Speed 7, Accuracy 8, Match Quality 6, Political Distortion 3 → IMM = (7×8×6)/3 = 112. Higher IMM means resources flow to their best use fast and cleanly; lower means politics or noise is deciding who gets what.
content.closing_statement: Let the rule decide, not the loudest voice.

caption:
Principle Nº 28: Internal Market Making

Scarce internal resources should flow toward highest-value uses, not the loudest politics. Scarce resources need a pricing mechanism or they get allocated by whoever complains loudest.

Ask yourself: When two priorities in your business compete for the same time, cash, or attention, what's the rule for who wins?
